courseTitle: Awareness of Mental Health and Dementia

items.html:
Everyone has mental health

Mental health is part of every person you support, and part of you too. It describes how we think, feel and cope with everyday life. Just as physical health moves up and down, so does mental health — some days we feel steady, other days we struggle. A person can live with a diagnosed condition and still have good days, meaningful relationships and a full life.

Mental health conditions are common. Many people will experience one at some point, whether a period of low mood after a loss or a long-standing condition managed over years. The people you care for are no different. Recognising this helps you offer support without surprise or judgement.

At this awareness level, your role is to recognise, respond and report. You are not expected to diagnose, label or treat — that is for qualified professionals.

Language and stigma

Stigma is the unfair judgement attached to mental health. It can stop people asking for help and leave them feeling ashamed or isolated. The words you choose either challenge stigma or reinforce it.

  • Use person-first language: say “a person experiencing psychosis”, not “a psychotic”.
  • Avoid casual labels: words like “mad”, “crazy” or “attention-seeking” are hurtful and dismissive.
  • Take distress seriously: never treat someone's feelings as a nuisance or an act.
  • Speak with, not about: include the person in conversations that concern them.

Challenging stigma is everyday work. When you treat a mental health condition with the same respect and openness as a physical one, you help create a culture where people feel safe to speak up and ask for support.

Conditions you may come across

You do not need clinical knowledge to support someone well, but a general awareness helps you notice when a person is struggling. Remember: these are things you might notice, not a checklist for diagnosis.

  • Anxiety: excessive worry, restlessness, a racing heart, avoiding situations, or seeking constant reassurance.
  • Depression: lasting low mood, loss of interest, tiredness, changes in appetite or sleep, and feelings of hopelessness.
  • Bipolar disorder: marked swings between very low periods and elevated, high-energy periods.
  • Psychosis: losing touch with shared reality — this can include hallucinations (sensing things others do not) or delusions (strongly held beliefs that are not based in fact).

Noticing and reporting

People express distress in different ways. Some withdraw and go quiet; others become agitated or tearful. A change from what is usual for that person often matters more than any single sign. You know the people you support — trust that knowledge.

  • Watch for change: a normally sociable person who stops joining in, or someone who suddenly cannot sleep.
  • Listen without fixing: you do not need answers; presence and attention help.
  • Record what you observe: note the facts, not your interpretation.
  • Report promptly: pass concerns to your senior, the nurse or the care plan so the right professional can act.

If someone talks about harming themselves or others, treat it seriously and escalate straight away. This is never something to keep to yourself.

items.question: A person you support usually enjoys the lunch club but has stopped attending, seems tearful and is not eating well. What is the most appropriate awareness-level response?

answers.text: Note the changes factually and report your concern to your senior or the care plan.
answers.feedback: Correct. Recognising a change from what is usual, recording it and reporting it is exactly your role at awareness level.

answers.text: Tell the person they are showing signs of depression so they can get treatment.
answers.feedback: Diagnosing is not your role. You recognise, respond and report — qualified professionals assess and diagnose.

answers.text: Wait a few weeks to see whether the low mood clears up by itself.
answers.feedback: Delaying could leave the person without support they need. A clear change should be reported promptly.

answers.text: Encourage the person to cheer up and join in as normal.
answers.feedback: This dismisses genuine distress. Listen and report rather than urging someone to simply feel better.

items.html:
Supporting good mental wellbeing

Good mental health is not just the absence of a condition. It grows from the everyday things that give life meaning: connection, purpose, activity and a sense of control. As a care worker you are well placed to nurture these in small, consistent ways.

  • Connection: a warm greeting, time to talk, and help to stay in touch with family and friends.
  • Purpose: chances to contribute, whether folding laundry, tending a plant or choosing the day's music.
  • Activity: movement, fresh air and hobbies that suit the person's interests and abilities.
  • Control: real choices over daily life, from what to wear to when to rise.

Small things, real difference

Wellbeing is built through relationships and routine, not grand gestures. When you take the time to learn what matters to a person and weave it into their day, you protect their sense of identity and belonging.

Notice what lifts someone and what drains them. A favourite radio programme, a walk in the garden, or a chat about grandchildren can steady a difficult day. Equally, rushing, noise or being talked over can wear a person down.

  • Offer choice, not commands: “Would you like tea or coffee?” respects autonomy.
  • Protect dignity: privacy, gentle language and unhurried care all matter.
  • Encourage strengths: focus on what a person can do, not only what they cannot.

Promoting wellbeing sits alongside spotting problems. Both keep the person, not the task, at the centre of your care.

items.question: Promoting positive mental health means only stepping in once a person shows signs of a mental health condition.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Wellbeing is built every day through connection, purpose, activity and choice — not only in response to problems.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Supporting wellbeing is proactive and ongoing, not just a reaction to signs of a condition.

items.html:
Understanding dementia

Dementia is not a single illness but a set of symptoms caused by different diseases that damage the brain. It is progressive, meaning it develops over time, and it is not a normal part of ageing. It affects memory, thinking, communication and the ability to carry out everyday tasks.

Dementia is caused by physical changes in the brain. It is not something a person chooses, and it cannot be reversed by trying harder. Understanding this helps you respond with patience rather than frustration when someone repeats a question or struggles with a familiar task.

At awareness level you do not diagnose dementia or judge its stage. You support the person, notice changes and report them to the nurse, senior or care plan so professional assessment can follow.

The main types

Different diseases cause dementia, and they can affect people in different ways. A person may even have more than one type at once.

  • Alzheimer's disease: the most common cause, usually beginning with memory difficulties and often progressing gradually.
  • Vascular dementia: caused by reduced blood flow to the brain, sometimes after a stroke, and may progress in noticeable steps.
  • Lewy body dementia: linked with changes in alertness, visual hallucinations and movement difficulties similar to Parkinson's.
  • Frontotemporal dementia: often affects younger people and can change personality, behaviour or language more than memory at first.

You do not need to identify which type someone has. Knowing that types differ simply reminds you to see each person as an individual whose experience will be their own.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete these facts about dementia.
items.textField: Dementia is *progressive* and is not a normal part of ageing. The most common cause is *Alzheimer's* disease, while a reduced blood supply to the brain causes *vascular* dementia. Visual hallucinations and changes in alertness are linked with *Lewy* body dementia.

items.html:
How dementia progresses

Dementia usually develops gradually, and every person's journey is different. Broadly, needs tend to increase over time, but there is no fixed timetable. Someone may stay independent in many areas for a long while, then need more support in others.

  • Memory: recent events may be forgotten while memories from long ago stay vivid.
  • Communication: finding the right words, following a conversation or reading may become harder.
  • Orientation: a person may lose track of the time, date or place, or become confused about where they are.
  • Everyday tasks: familiar activities such as cooking or dressing can take more time and support.

What does not change

It is easy to focus on loss, but a great deal remains. Above all, the person is still there. Their character, their history and their capacity to feel do not disappear.

  • The person: their identity, humour and personality remain part of who they are.
  • Feelings: people living with dementia feel joy, love, fear, boredom and grief just as anyone does — often more strongly.
  • The need for connection: warmth, respect and companionship matter as much as ever.
  • Emotional memory: a person may forget an event but still remember how it made them feel.

This is why how you make someone feel is so important. A kind tone and a calm presence reach a person even when words are lost. Care that protects feelings protects the person.

items.question: A person living with dementia may forget a conversation but still be left with the feeling it gave them.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Emotional memory often outlasts factual memory, which is why a warm, respectful manner matters so much.
items.wrongMsg: Actually this is true. People often retain the feeling an interaction created even after forgetting the event itself.

items.html:
Seeing the person, not the diagnosis

A diagnosis of dementia describes part of a person's health — it does not describe who they are. Behind every diagnosis is a whole life: a childhood, a career perhaps, relationships, achievements, losses and passions. Person-centred care means keeping that individual, not the condition, at the heart of everything you do.

When you know someone's story, care becomes easier and kinder. Knowing that a man was a lifelong gardener helps you understand why he wants to be outside. Knowing that a woman raised four children helps you see why she worries about “the little ones” in the afternoon. Their words make sense in the context of their life.

Using life history and identity

Life-history work is not just pleasant background — it is a practical tool for good care. Many services use a life-story book, a “this is me” document or a similar record. Read it, add to it, and use it.

  • Learn what matters: preferred name, routines, faith, food, music and past occupation.
  • Respect identity: support people to dress, groom and live in ways that feel like themselves.
  • Involve families: relatives hold precious knowledge and can help you understand the person.
  • Connect through interests: a shared love of football or a favourite hymn can open real communication.

When identity is honoured, people are calmer, more engaged and more themselves. Seeing the person behind the diagnosis is the foundation of everything that follows.

items.question: Why is knowing a person's life history useful when supporting someone living with dementia?

answers.text: It helps you understand the person's needs, wishes and behaviour, and connect with them meaningfully.
answers.feedback: Correct. Life history is a practical tool that makes care more personal, more understandable and more effective.

answers.text: It lets you decide which stage of dementia the person has reached.
answers.feedback: Assessing stages is not your role. Life history is about knowing the person, not judging their condition.

answers.text: It is interesting background but has little effect on day-to-day care.
answers.feedback: Life history has a real, practical impact — it helps you understand behaviour and communicate well.

answers.text: It allows you to correct the person whenever their memories are inaccurate.
answers.feedback: The aim is to connect and reassure, not to correct. Life history helps you understand, not to challenge someone's reality.

items.html:
Communication that connects

Good communication is the heart of supporting someone with a mental health condition or dementia. It is about far more than words — your tone, pace, face and body all send messages. When words become difficult, these become even more important.

  • Give time: slow down, pause, and allow the person space to respond without pressure.
  • Keep it simple: short sentences and one idea at a time are easier to follow.
  • Get down to eye level: approach calmly from the front and make gentle eye contact.
  • Watch non-verbal cues: a frown, a turned shoulder or a smile tells you a great deal.

Listening and reassuring

Listening well shows respect and builds trust. Let the person lead where you can, and value what they are trying to express even if the words are muddled.

  • Listen actively: nod, reflect back what you hear, and show you are interested.
  • Avoid arguing: if someone believes something that is not accurate, focus on the feeling behind it rather than correcting the facts.
  • Reduce distractions: turn down background noise so the person can concentrate.
  • Offer reassurance: a calm voice and a warm manner can ease worry when words cannot.

Communication is a two-way relationship. When you adapt to the person — their pace, their preferred name, their good time of day — you help them stay connected, understood and valued. If communication needs change, report it so the care plan stays up to date.

items.question: A person living with dementia insists it is time to collect her children from school, though they are grown adults. What is the most person-centred response?

answers.text: Respond calmly to the feeling behind her words, offering reassurance rather than correcting the facts.
answers.feedback: Correct. Acknowledging the emotion and reassuring her respects her reality and reduces distress.

answers.text: Explain firmly that her children are adults now and there is no school to go to.
answers.feedback: Bluntly correcting the facts can cause distress and confusion. Focus on the feeling, not the accuracy.

answers.text: Ignore what she says and carry on with the task at hand.
answers.feedback: Ignoring the person dismisses a real feeling. She needs acknowledgement and reassurance.

answers.text: Tell her she is confused because of her dementia.
answers.feedback: This is neither kind nor helpful. Connect with the emotion behind her words instead.

items.html:
The environment matters

Surroundings have a powerful effect on how a person feels and copes. A confusing or overwhelming space can add to distress, while a calm, familiar one can settle it. Small adjustments often make a real difference.

  • Reduce noise and clutter: too much noise or busyness can overload and unsettle a person.
  • Good lighting: bright, even light reduces shadows that can be misread or frightening.
  • Familiar cues: clear signs, familiar objects and personal belongings help with orientation and comfort.
  • Safe to move: space to walk freely supports independence and eases restlessness.

Meaningful activity

Everyone needs purpose and enjoyment. Meaningful activity — occupation that connects with who a person is — supports wellbeing, reduces boredom and can lessen distress. It does not have to be organised or elaborate.

  • Match interests and abilities: build on hobbies, past work and things the person still enjoys and can do.
  • Focus on the doing, not the result: the pleasure of folding towels or sorting buttons matters more than a perfect outcome.
  • Use the senses: music, textures, scents, gardening and gentle movement can all engage and soothe.
  • Keep it dignified: activities should feel adult and purposeful, never childish.

Boredom, loneliness and a lack of purpose are common causes of distress. By shaping a calm environment and offering meaningful occupation, you support wellbeing and often prevent difficulties before they begin.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete these points about environment and activity.
items.textField: A calm environment with less *noise* and good *lighting* can ease distress. Offering meaningful *activity* that matches a person's interests supports wellbeing, because boredom and *loneliness* are common causes of distress.

items.html:
Understanding distress reactions

You may hear terms like “challenging behaviour”, but this language is unhelpful and unfair. It blames the person and misses the point. Instead we speak of distress reactions: understandable responses from a person who is upset, frightened or trying to meet a need they cannot easily express.

When someone becomes agitated, tearful, resistant or restless, they are communicating. Behind almost every distress reaction is an unmet need. The behaviour is not the problem to be managed — it is a signal to be understood.

Your task is to look beyond what you see and ask: what is this person trying to tell me? Answering that question with curiosity and compassion is far more effective than trying to stop the behaviour itself.

Finding the unmet need

Needs come in many forms, and they are often practical. Working through the possibilities calmly helps you find the cause.

  • Physical: pain, hunger, thirst, needing the toilet, being too hot or cold, or tiredness.
  • Emotional: fear, boredom, loneliness, grief or feeling ignored.
  • Environmental: noise, crowding, unfamiliar surroundings or too much going on.
  • Communication: not being understood, or not understanding what is happening.

Stay calm, keep yourself and the person safe, and give them space if they need it. Do not take distress personally. Afterwards, record what happened, what came before it and what helped, then report it — patterns you notice can guide the care plan. Pain in particular is easily missed and should always be considered and reported.

items.question: A distress reaction is usually a way of communicating an unmet need rather than deliberate difficult behaviour.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Distress reactions signal an unmet need — physical, emotional or environmental — and call for understanding, not blame.
items.wrongMsg: Actually this is true. Distress reactions communicate an unmet need; your role is to understand and respond to it.

items.html:
Delirium: a medical emergency

Delirium is a sudden change in a person's mental state, usually developing over hours or days. It is not the same as dementia, though the two can occur together. Crucially, delirium is a sign that something is physically wrong, and it is a medical emergency that needs urgent attention.

The key clue is sudden change from how the person normally is. Look for:

  • New or worsening confusion: far more muddled than usual, coming on quickly.
  • Changes in alertness: drowsy and hard to rouse, or restless and agitated.
  • Fluctuation: symptoms that come and go through the day, often worse later on.
  • New hallucinations or fear: seeing things or becoming suddenly frightened.

When and how to escalate

Common causes of delirium include infection (such as a urine or chest infection), dehydration, constipation, pain and medication. Many are treatable, which is exactly why fast action matters — and why you must never dismiss sudden confusion as “just their dementia”.

  • Act quickly: report a sudden change to your senior or the nurse straight away.
  • Seek medical help: the person needs urgent review — contact the GP or 111, and call 999 if they are seriously unwell.
  • Keep them safe and calm: reassure, reduce noise, and stay with them where you can.
  • Record the facts: when the change started and what you noticed helps professionals act.

Recognising delirium and escalating without delay can save a life. When in doubt, always raise your concern.

items.question: A person who is normally alert becomes suddenly and increasingly confused over a few hours, seeing things that are not there. What should you do?

answers.text: Treat it as a possible medical emergency and escalate urgently to your senior, the nurse or medical help.
answers.feedback: Correct. Sudden confusion may be delirium, a medical emergency needing urgent escalation — never dismiss it as 'just dementia'.

answers.text: Assume it is a normal part of their dementia and monitor over the coming week.
answers.feedback: A sudden change is not normal and should never be assumed to be dementia. Delirium needs urgent action.

answers.text: Wait until the next scheduled GP visit to mention it.
answers.feedback: Waiting is dangerous. Delirium is a medical emergency requiring prompt escalation, not a routine mention.

answers.text: Encourage the person to rest and see if they are better tomorrow.
answers.feedback: Delay could be harmful. Sudden confusion must be escalated urgently for medical review.

items.html:
Awareness of the Mental Health Act 1983

The Mental Health Act 1983 is the main law in England and Wales that covers the assessment, care and treatment of people with a mental health condition, including where this may need to happen without their consent. You are not expected to apply this law — this is awareness only — but understanding its purpose helps you support people's rights.

The Act's central purpose is to allow people to be assessed or treated for a mental health condition when this is necessary for their own health or safety, or for the protection of others, and when they may not agree to it. Being formally detained under the Act is often called being “sectioned”.

Safeguards and your role

The Act is not only about detention. It also builds in protections to make sure people are treated lawfully, fairly and with dignity.

  • Safeguards: there are rules about who can make decisions, rights to appeal and access to independent support.
  • Least restriction: the aim is always to use the least restrictive option that keeps the person and others safe.
  • Rights remain: a person subject to the Act keeps their dignity, their voice and their right to be treated with respect.
  • Your part: you provide day-to-day care, treat the person as an individual, and report concerns — you do not make or enforce legal decisions.

At awareness level, the key message is simple: know that this law exists to protect and support people, and keep treating everyone with the same compassion and respect.

items.question: At awareness level, care workers are expected to decide when a person should be detained under the Mental Health Act 1983.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Legal decisions under the Act are made by qualified professionals. Your role is day-to-day care, respect and reporting concerns.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Care workers do not make detention decisions — those rest with qualified professionals. Your role is care and reporting.

items.html:
Crossover with the Mental Capacity Act 2005

Mental health law does not stand alone. Many people you support are also protected by the Mental Capacity Act 2005 (MCA), which is about making decisions when a person may not be able to make a particular decision for themselves. A person living with dementia or a mental health condition may still have capacity for many decisions — capacity is specific to each decision and each moment.

The MCA works on key principles: assume capacity unless proven otherwise, support people to decide for themselves, and where someone cannot decide, act in their best interests in the least restrictive way. Where a person needs to be deprived of their liberty to keep them safe, the Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS) provide legal protection and oversight.

Advocacy, carers and going further

People do not face these situations alone, and neither do you.

  • Advocacy: an independent advocate helps a person express their wishes and understand their rights, especially when they have little other support.
  • Carers: family and friends often know the person best; involve and value them, and remember they need support too.
  • Your role: respect choices, report concerns about capacity or restrictions, and follow the care plan.

This page is an introduction only. The Mental Capacity Act, best interests, DoLS and the wider Liberty Protection framework are detailed areas in their own right. For proper depth, complete the separate Mental Capacity Act course, which covers these duties fully. Here, simply be aware that these safeguards exist and that they protect the rights of the people you care for.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete these points about the Mental Capacity Act and related safeguards.
items.textField: The Mental Capacity Act 2005 assumes a person has *capacity* unless shown otherwise. Where someone cannot make a decision, others must act in their best *interests* in the least restrictive way. An independent *advocate* can help a person express their wishes, and the Deprivation of Liberty *Safeguards* provide legal oversight when someone's freedom must be restricted.

items.html:
Recognise, respond, report

Across this course, one thread runs through everything: your role is to recognise, respond and report. You bring awareness, kindness and good observation — not diagnosis or treatment.

  • Recognise: notice changes in mood, behaviour or wellbeing, and know that a sudden change may be delirium — a medical emergency.
  • Respond: communicate calmly, protect dignity, meet needs, and treat distress reactions as signals of an unmet need rather than difficult behaviour.
  • Report: record what you observe factually and pass concerns to your senior, the nurse or the care plan so the right professional can act.

Your role and its limits

Supporting people with mental health conditions and dementia is skilled, human work. It rests on seeing the person behind any diagnosis, honouring their history and feelings, and challenging stigma through the language and respect you offer every day.

Know your limits too. You do not diagnose, you do not make legal decisions under the Mental Health Act, and you do not carry serious concerns alone.

  • Escalate promptly: report distress, decline or risk without delay.
  • Seek medical help: use the GP, 111, or 999 for emergencies such as suspected delirium or risk of harm.
  • Keep learning: complete the separate Mental Capacity Act course for the law in depth.

When you recognise, respond and report with compassion, you help people live with dignity, safety and a sense of themselves — whatever they are facing.
